summary: Shaw describes his teaching method of having beginners write comments first, then fill in code underneath—a technique he calls converting complex problems into 'paint-by-numbers' problems. He applies this same principle across disciplines: coding, writing, and painting all benefit from outlining before executing. He then proposes a code editor that would automate this process, using machine learning to suggest code beneath comments while continuously running tests. The editor would essentially formalize what programmers already do—searching Stack Overflow and documentation—into an integrated, intelligent workflow. Written in 2015, the idea presciently describes something close to modern AI coding assistants.
key_points: Beginners know what they want code to do but can't bridge the gap to implementation, so starting with English descriptions (comments) gives them a foothold
The comment-first, fill-in-the-blanks approach is universal across creative disciplines—coding, writing, and painting all benefit from outlining before executing
Solving complex problems in any medium means converting them into paint-by-numbers problems: breaking the whole into tiny solvable pieces
A code editor could use machine learning and curated code catalogs to auto-suggest implementations beneath comments while continuously running tests
This would formalize what programmers already do informally—searching Stack Overflow and docs using the same words they'd put in a comment
money_quotes: They don't have enough experience to close the gap, but since they can describe what they want the function to do then that's their start.
I find that starting with desired results works best for beginners and early coders.
In general, the way you can solve a complex problem that's difficult to visualize in any medium (code, words, paint, music, etc.) is to convert the problem to a paint-by-numbers problem.
Instead of just trying to do it all at once right in your head and get it right, you break it down into tiny problems, then solve each one.
Why not have the editor use fancy machine learning algorithms and a vast catalog of existing curated code to do this for you?
Just a random idea, but could be an interesting thing to research. Call it 'Comment Driven Coding' for lack of a better name.
key_insight: Complex creative problems in any medium—code, writing, painting—are best solved by decomposing them into small, describable pieces and filling in the blanks, a process that machines could eventually automate.
